text:
MEDICAL ONCOLOGY CONSULTATION NOTE
Department of Medical Oncology

Patient: Gerald T. Holloway
Age: 58 years
Referring Service: Colorectal Surgery / General Surgery
Reason for Consultation: New diagnosis of metastatic colorectal cancer, Stage IVA; evaluation for systemic therapy following surgical resection of primary tumor

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT

Mr. Holloway is a 58-year-old male with newly diagnosed Stage IVA sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous liver metastases, presenting for initial medical oncology consultation approximately 5 weeks following laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy. He is referred for discussion of systemic therapy options and coordination of multidisciplinary care.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Mr. Holloway initially presented to his primary care physician approximately 6-7 weeks ago with several months of progressive lower abdominal cramping, a change in bowel habits (alternating loose stools and constipation), and intermittent hematochezia. He denied significant unintentional weight loss at initial presentation, though he endorses approximately 8 lbs of weight loss over the past 3 months in retrospect. He had not undergone prior colonoscopy screening.

Colonoscopy was performed and revealed a partially obstructing mass in the sigmoid colon. Colonoscopic biopsies confirmed moderately differentiated adenocarcinoma. Staging CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis with contrast demonstrated the primary sigmoid mass measuring 4.8 x 3.9 cm, along with 4 hepatic metastatic lesions -- the largest measuring 3.1 cm in hepatic segment VI, with additional lesions in segments IVb (2.2 cm), II (1.4 cm), and VII (0.9 cm). No pulmonary metastases, peritoneal carcinomatosis, or extrahepatic abdominal disease was identified at the time of staging.

Molecular profiling of the colonoscopic biopsy specimen was obtained prior to surgery: KRAS p.G12D mutation confirmed (variant allele frequency 41%), NRAS wild-type, BRAF V600E wild-type. MSI/MMR testing by IHC demonstrated intact mismatch repair protein expression (MLH1+, MSH2+, MSH6+, PMS2+), consistent with a microsatellite-stable (MSS) tumor. HER2 was not amplified. Comprehensive NGS on the same specimen confirmed KRAS G12D as the primary oncogenic driver; co-mutations included APC p.R1450* (loss of function, VAF 44%), TP53 p.R175H (gain-of-function, VAF 39%), and copy number gains in MYC and CDK8. TMB was low at 6 mut/Mb. No actionable fusions were identified.

Initial tumor markers: CEA 187 ng/mL, CA 19-9 94 U/mL.

After multidisciplinary review, given the synchronous hepatic metastatic disease and without evidence of peritoneal spread or pulmonary involvement, the colorectal surgery team proceeded with laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis approximately 5 weeks ago. The procedure was uncomplicated. Final surgical pathology demonstrated pT3 pN2a (3 of 18 lymph nodes positive for metastatic adenocarcinoma), R0 resection with proximal and distal margins well clear and radial margin 4 mm. Lymphovascular invasion was extensive; perineural invasion was present; one tumor deposit was identified. Tumor budding was Bd3 (high).

He has recovered well from surgery. He denies wound complications, fever, anastomotic leak symptoms, or ileus. He reports a return to near-normal bowel function and diet. He notes mild fatigue that he attributes to the post-operative recovery period. No pain issues currently; he has weaned off analgesics.

The patient understands that his liver metastases were not resected at the time of the primary surgery given the number and distribution of lesions, and he is eager to discuss the plan for systemic treatment. He has been evaluated in informal consultation with hepatobiliary surgery during his post-operative period and was told that resection of the liver metastases may be feasible after a response to systemic therapy. He is highly motivated to pursue active treatment.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Constitutional: Mild fatigue, post-operative in nature; 8 lb weight loss over past 3 months (preceding diagnosis). No fevers or chills.
Gastrointestinal: Bowel function recovering -- soft/formed stools, 1-2 per day. No hematochezia since surgery. No nausea, vomiting, or abdominal pain currently. Appetite returning to near-baseline.
Neurological: No peripheral neuropathy, no cognitive changes. Denies headaches.
Cardiovascular: Known hypertension, on medication. Denies chest pain, palpitations, dyspnea on exertion beyond expected post-operative deconditioning.
Respiratory: No cough, hemoptysis, or shortness of breath at rest.
Musculoskeletal: No bone pain, no arthralgias.
Dermatological: No rashes.
Genitourinary: No hematuria, dysuria, or obstructive symptoms.
Hematologic: No easy bruising or bleeding. No prior VTE history.
Endocrine/metabolic: No known diabetes. Well-controlled hyperlipidemia.
Psychiatric: Anxious regarding diagnosis but coping; supportive family at home.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY

1. Hypertension -- diagnosed approximately 10 years ago; well-controlled on lisinopril 10 mg daily prior to cancer diagnosis
2. Hyperlipidemia -- managed with atorvastatin 40 mg daily
3. Former smoker -- quit 12 years prior; 20 pack-year history; no known COPD or pulmonary sequelae
4. Sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma, Stage IVA (as described above) -- newly diagnosed
5. No prior colonoscopy; was not up to date on colorectal cancer screening
6. No prior abdominal surgery prior to this colectomy
7. No personal or family history of colorectal cancer, Lynch syndrome, or hereditary polyposis syndromes

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
1. Laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with primary anastomosis -- approximately 5 weeks ago; uncomplicated

---

SOCIAL HISTORY

Mr. Holloway is a 58-year-old male who works as a civil engineer. He is married and lives with his wife. He has two adult children who are aware of his diagnosis and are supportive. He is a former smoker (quit 12 years ago, approximately 20 pack-year history). He drinks alcohol occasionally -- 1-2 drinks per week socially, no history of heavy use. No illicit drug use. He is physically active at baseline (walked 2-3 miles daily prior to diagnosis) though has been more sedentary in the post-operative period. He has adequate social support and health insurance. He and his wife are engaged and asking thoughtful questions. He expresses a preference for aggressive treatment with intent to pursue potential resection of liver metastases if feasible. He works full-time and would prefer a treatment schedule that allows him to maintain some level of professional functioning if possible.

---

FAMILY HISTORY

No family history of colorectal cancer. Father deceased at age 74 (coronary artery disease). Mother alive at age 81 (hypertension, type 2 diabetes). One sibling (brother, age 54) with no known malignancy. No family history of breast, ovarian, pancreatic, or gastric cancer. No known hereditary cancer syndromes. Patient counseled that given MSS/proficient MMR status and no family history, Lynch syndrome is unlikely; no germline testing recommended at this time by genetics (per multidisciplinary discussion), though patient was offered referral if desired.

---

ALLERGIES

NKDA (no known drug allergies). No prior chemotherapy exposures to document.

---

MEDICATIONS (Current)

1. Lisinopril 10 mg orally once daily (hypertension)
2. Atorvastatin 40 mg orally once daily at bedtime (hyperlipidemia)
3. Aspirin 81 mg orally once daily (cardiovascular prophylaxis -- to be discussed re: perioperative/chemotherapy context)
4. Acetaminophen 500 mg orally every 6 hours as needed (post-operative pain, tapering)
5. Docusate sodium 100 mg orally twice daily (post-operative bowel regimen)

Vitamins/Supplements: Multivitamin daily. No herbal supplements. No anticoagulants.

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals: Blood pressure 138/86 mmHg, heart rate 74 bpm (regular), respiratory rate 16/min, temperature 98.4°F, SpO2 98% on room air, weight 81.2 kg, height 178 cm, BSA approximately 1.97 m2, BMI 25.6 kg/m2.

General: Well-appearing male in no acute distress. Alert, oriented, well-nourished. Engaged and cooperative. Mild anxious affect, appropriate to clinical context.

HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic. Sclerae non-icteric. Mucous membranes moist. No oral mucositis. No lymphadenopathy in the cervical or supraclavicular regions.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm. Normal S1 and S2. No murmurs, rubs, or gallops. No JVD. Peripheral pulses intact bilaterally.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No crackles, wheezes, or rhonchi. Good air movement throughout.

Abdomen: Soft, mildly tender over the left lower quadrant laparoscopic port sites, healing well. Well-healed laparoscopic incisions. No midline incision. No hepatosplenomegaly appreciated on palpation. No ascites. No palpable masses. Bowel sounds normoactive.

Extremities: No peripheral edema. No calf tenderness or swelling bilaterally. Distal pulses 2+ throughout.

Neurological: Alert and oriented x3. Cranial nerves grossly intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation grossly intact to light touch in bilateral upper and lower extremities. No ataxia on finger-to-nose testing. Gait steady.

Dermatological: No rash, jaundice, or pallor. Skin turgor normal.

Lymph nodes: No palpable axillary, inguinal, or peripheral lymphadenopathy.

ECOG Performance Status: 1 (restricted in physically strenuous activity but ambulatory and able to carry out work of a light or sedentary nature).

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

Complete Blood Count (recent post-operative check):
- WBC: 7.2 x10^3/uL
- Hemoglobin: 11.4 g/dL (mild post-operative anemia; iron studies pending)
- Hematocrit: 34.8%
- Platelets: 318 x10^3/uL
- ANC: 4,100/uL

Comprehensive Metabolic Panel:
- Na: 139 mEq/L, K: 4.1 mEq/L, Cl: 101 mEq/L, CO2: 25 mEq/L
- BUN: 14 mg/dL, Creatinine: 0.94 mg/dL, eGFR >60 mL/min/1.73m2
- Glucose: 98 mg/dL
- Total bilirubin: 0.7 mg/dL, Direct bilirubin: 0.2 mg/dL
- ALT: 38 U/L, AST: 31 U/L (mildly elevated, attributable to hepatic metastases but within acceptable range)
- Alkaline phosphatase: 112 U/L (mildly elevated)
- Albumin: 3.7 g/dL

Tumor Markers:
- CEA: 187 ng/mL (at diagnosis; most recent post-operative level pending -- drawn today)
- CA 19-9: 94 U/mL (at diagnosis; most recent pending)

Coagulation: PT/INR 1.1, PTT 28 sec (within normal limits).

Lipid panel (recent): LDL 82 mg/dL on atorvastatin (well-controlled).

Urine dipstick: Negative protein, no hematuria.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

Staging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast (approximately 6 weeks ago, at diagnosis):
Primary sigmoid colon mass 4.8 x 3.9 cm with partial luminal narrowing; 4 hepatic hypodense metastatic lesions as described (largest 3.1 cm, segment VI). No peritoneal implants, no pulmonary metastases, no mediastinal lymphadenopathy. Stage IVA disease.

No new imaging available since surgery; restaging CT planned after initial systemic therapy cycles.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Mr. Holloway is a 58-year-old male civil engineer, ECOG PS 1, with Stage IVA moderately differentiated sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with synchronous hepatic metastases (4 lesions), now approximately 5 weeks post-laparoscopic sigmoid colectomy with R0 resection (pT3 pN2a). Molecular profile: KRAS p.G12D mutant, NRAS/BRAF wild-type, MSS/pMMR, HER2 non-amplified, TMB-low. He is here today to discuss systemic therapy for his metastatic disease.

1. METASTATIC COLORECTAL CANCER -- SYSTEMIC THERAPY DISCUSSION

Given his molecular profile, several important biomarker-driven exclusions apply:
- KRAS G12D mutation renders him ineligible for anti-EGFR therapy (Erbitux, Vectibix/pani). This applies regardless of tumor sidedness.
- MSS/pMMR status excludes him from benefit with immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembro, Opdivo) per current evidence base for CRC.
- BRAF wild-type means the BRAF-MEK targeted doublet (Braftovi/Mektovi + cetux) is not applicable.
- KRAS G12D-specific inhibitors remain investigational and are not yet commercially available; clinical trial enrollment could be revisited at later disease stages if available.
- No actionable fusions or other targets were identified on comprehensive NGS.

For first-line treatment of metastatic colorectal cancer in a patient with good PS and resectable or potentially convertible hepatic disease, standard-of-care systemic therapy consists of a doublet chemotherapy backbone with an anti-VEGF/VEGFR agent. I discussed the following regimens:

- FOLFOX (oxali + leucovorin + 5-FU) plus bev (Avastin)
- FOLFIRI (iri + leucovorin + 5-FU) plus Avastin
- CAPOX (cape + oxali) plus bev

After thorough discussion with Mr. Holloway and his wife, I am recommending modified FOLFOX6 plus Avastin as the first-line regimen. Rationale:
- FOLFOX backbone has demonstrated favorable efficacy and tolerability in the first-line metastatic CRC setting; oxali-based regimens are standard in conversion therapy intent given data supporting higher hepatic resection rates
- bev (anti-VEGF) has been well-validated in combination with FOLFOX for overall survival benefit in metastatic CRC regardless of RAS mutation status
- CAPOX is a reasonable alternative but IV infusional 5-FU (FOLFOX) gives us the flexibility to dose-modify each component individually; pump-delivered continuous infusion 5-FU is familiar to this patient and preferred by our nursing team
- Mr. Holloway has adequate cardiac, renal, and hepatic function to tolerate Avastin; blood pressure is borderline elevated and will be closely monitored and managed

Regimen (planned):
- Eloxatin 85 mg/m2 IV day 1
- Leucovorin 400 mg/m2 IV day 1
- 5FU 400 mg/m2 IV bolus day 1
- 5-FU 2400 mg/m2 IV continuous infusion over 46 hours (via portable pump)
- bev 5 mg/kg IV day 1
- Cycle length: 14 days (every 2 weeks)
- Treatment intent: Disease control with goal of achieving sufficient hepatic response to render the liver metastases resectable; plan for reassessment with hepatobiliary surgery after 6-8 cycles

I spent considerable time reviewing expected toxicities with Mr. Holloway and his wife:
- Peripheral sensory neuropathy (Eloxatin-related, cumulative): cold-induced acral paresthesias early on; progressive sensory changes with prolonged use -- I counseled him to avoid cold exposure, particularly cold liquids and cold environmental exposure in the first 24-48 hours after each cycle. Neuropathy is the primary dose-limiting toxicity of Eloxatin and may require dose reduction or discontinuation with cumulative treatment.
- Nausea and vomiting: typically well-managed with modern antiemetics (ondansetron, prochlorperazine, dexamethasone)
- Myelosuppression: CBC will be monitored prior to each cycle; he will be instructed regarding neutropenic precautions and fever reporting
- Fatigue: common; likely to worsen with cumulative cycles
- Mucositis/hand-foot syndrome: less prominent with IV 5-FU than oral Xeloda
- Avastin-specific risks:
  * Hypertension: Mr. Holloway already has borderline elevated blood pressure on lisinopril 10 mg; Avastin frequently worsens hypertension and we will escalate antihypertensive therapy as needed. Close BP monitoring at each visit.
  * Wound healing impairment: bev must be held until adequate post-operative wound healing. Given he is approximately 5 weeks post-surgery and wounds appear well-healed, I am comfortable initiating bev at cycle 1. Standard of practice requires at least 4-6 weeks post-operatively; we meet this threshold.
  * Thromboembolism: both arterial (stroke, MI) and venous (DVT, PE) risks are modestly elevated with bev. Patient will be educated on signs/symptoms. Low-dose aspirin was discussed; I will defer to surgical team regarding resumption timing.
  * Proteinuria: urine dipstick monitoring each cycle; if significant proteinuria develops, dose hold per protocol.
  * GI perforation/fistula: rare but serious; patient instructed to report any acute abdominal pain, particularly given anastomosis site. Bowel continuity appears intact at this time and there were no intraoperative complications.
  * Bleeding: rare epistaxis is most common; serious bleeding uncommon in CRC population without major coagulopathy
- I also discussed hair thinning (mild with FOLFOX vs. prominent with iri) and the cumulative fatigue of bi-weekly therapy

Patient verbalized understanding and agreed to proceed. Informed consent obtained and documented.

2. HEPATOBILIARY MULTIDISCIPLINARY REVIEW

I have formally reviewed the pre-operative and current hepatic imaging with Dr. [hepatobiliary surgery attending] in the context of the multidisciplinary tumor board. The four hepatic lesions are potentially amenable to curative-intent resection if adequate oncologic response is achieved and future liver remnant (FLR) is preserved. Currently, the lesion distribution (bilobar, 4 lesions) and the need to preserve sufficient hepatic parenchyma makes upfront resection unfavorable without cytoreduction. After 6-8 cycles of systemic therapy with an adequate response, repeat imaging will be obtained and hepatobiliary surgery will re-evaluate. Portal vein embolization and staged hepatectomy will be discussed at that time as needed. This goal of potential surgical conversion is a key driver of the chosen regimen and treatment intensity.

3. TUMOR MARKERS

Baseline CEA 187 ng/mL and CA 19-9 94 U/mL are established. Both will be monitored at each cycle and are expected to trend down with effective therapy. Rising markers in the context of clinical stability should prompt consideration of earlier restaging imaging.

4. SUPPORTIVE CARE AND ANCILLARY REFERRALS

- Antiemetics: Ondansetron 8 mg IV pre-chemotherapy, dexamethasone 12 mg IV day 1. Take-home ondansetron 8 mg orally every 8 hours days 1-3, prochlorperazine 10 mg orally as needed for breakthrough nausea.
- Bowel: Loperamide on hand for diarrhea management. Continue fiber-rich diet as tolerated.
- Nutritional support: Mild albumin decline (3.7 g/dL) noted; referred to oncology dietitian for optimization of protein and caloric intake.
- Social work: Referral placed for social work support given new Stage IV diagnosis, occupational concerns, and financial/insurance navigation for bev (Avastin is typically covered under Part B/commercial plans; patient navigator will assist).
- Palliative care: Discussed the role of palliative care as a collaborative partner in symptom management -- not synonymous with hospice. Early integration palliative care referral placed per our institutional protocol for patients initiating systemic therapy for metastatic disease.
- Hypertension management: Will monitor BP at every cycle visit. I am not escalating antihypertensive therapy today given BP of 138/86 mmHg is borderline; however, patient instructed to monitor BP at home twice daily for the first 4 weeks on bev. Will work with primary care or escalate within oncology service as needed.
- Aspirin: Deferred; will discuss with surgery regarding safe timing to resume.
- Low molecular weight heparin (LMWH) VTE prophylaxis: Given Stage IV cancer, will discuss Khorana score and individualized VTE risk at first treatment visit; institutional guideline supports considering prophylactic anticoagulation in high-risk patients -- will revisit after surgery team sign-off.

5. FERTILITY / GONADAL TOXICITY

Mr. Holloway is 58 years old and does not express concern regarding fertility preservation. This was briefly discussed and he declined further evaluation.

6. TREATMENT SCHEDULE

Cycle 1 will be scheduled approximately 1 week from today to allow time for insurance authorization, placement of a port (or use of existing IV access -- patient currently has PICC line placed by surgery team, which will be used for cycle 1; port placement will be scheduled thereafter), and pre-treatment labs. Baseline CBC, CMP, LFTs, urine dipstick, CEA, and CA 19-9 will be obtained on cycle 1 day 1 prior to administration.

Restaging CT chest/abdomen/pelvis will be performed after approximately cycle 6 (3 months of therapy), with interim evaluation by hepatobiliary surgery at that time.

7. GOALS OF CARE

I had a direct and compassionate conversation with Mr. Holloway and his wife regarding the nature of Stage IVA metastatic colorectal cancer. I was honest that the goal of systemic therapy at this stage is primarily to control disease, reduce tumor burden, and potentially render the liver metastases resectable -- which, if achieved, could substantially improve long-term outcomes. I also discussed that cure with systemic therapy alone is unlikely, though durable disease control is achievable in a meaningful subset of patients. Mr. Holloway expressed a clear preference for aggressive treatment and a desire to explore all options to achieve resection. He is emotionally engaged, asking appropriate questions, and has a supportive family present. He understands this is a marathon, not a sprint, and is committed to participating in a longitudinal treatment program.

Follow-up: Cycle 1 of modified FOLFOX6 + Avastin scheduled as above. Will be seen in clinic on day 1 of each cycle. 24-hour oncology on-call number provided for urgent concerns including fever, uncontrolled nausea/vomiting, severe abdominal pain, or signs of infection.

Electronically signed,
[Attending Medical Oncologist, MD]
Department of Medical Oncology
Time spent: Approximately 65 minutes (consultation, review of records, counseling, coordination of care)
